column2: By HTTP convention, which statement correctly describes GET vs. POST?
column3: GET may freely alter server state; POST must not.
column4: GET must not alter server state; POST typically does.
column5: Both GET and POST may alter server state freely.
column6: GET is for dynamic content; POST is for static content.

column2: A user requests http://example.com/news?id=42 and the server computes the page from a database. Which statement is correct?
column3: This is static content because the URL does not change between requests.
column4: This is dynamic content; the server generates it on the fly per request.
column5: Static content must always be served by Nginx; dynamic content by Apache.
column6: The '?' in the URL means the response is always cached.

column2: What problem does WSGI (Web Server Gateway Interface) solve?
column3: It encrypts HTTP traffic between the client and the web server.
column4: It defines a standard calling convention so a web server can invoke a Python web framework to handle dynamic requests.
column5: It manages database connection pooling for web applications.
column6: It compresses HTTP headers to reduce bandwidth usage.

column2: HTTP is stateless. What must a web application do to remember a logged-in user across multiple requests?
column3: Nothing — HTTP automatically tracks sessions via the underlying TCP connection.
column4: Store session state explicitly (e.g. via cookies) because HTTP itself carries no session context between requests.
column5: Use HTTP/2, which adds built-in stateful sessions.
column6: Configure the web server to maintain sessions transparently; the framework need not worry.

column2: In the JavaScript event loop model, what happens while a callback function is executing?
column3: Other callbacks can interrupt it if a higher-priority event arrives.
column4: The event loop blocks on new events until the callback returns; each callback runs to completion without interruption.
column5: The callback runs in a separate OS thread.
column6: The browser pauses all rendering but continues processing network events.

column2: A developer calls fetch('/api/data') and assumes the returned Promise rejects when the server responds with HTTP 404. This assumption is:
column3: Correct — fetch rejects on any non-2xx HTTP status code.
column4: Incorrect — fetch only rejects on a network failure. HTTP 404 still resolves; the developer must check response.ok explicitly.
column5: Correct for fetch but not for the older XMLHttpRequest API.
column6: Incorrect — fetch automatically retries on 404 before resolving.

column2: What is the primary motivation for using JavaScript Promises instead of plain nested callbacks?
column3: Promises execute faster because they run in a dedicated thread pool.
column4: Promises allow chaining with .then() and async/await, avoiding deeply nested callback hell.
column5: Promises guarantee the event arrives within a fixed time bound.
column6: Promises automatically retry failed network requests.

column2: HTTP/1.1 browsers open multiple parallel TCP connections to overcome head-of-line blocking. How does HTTP/2 address this differently?
column3: HTTP/2 removes the concept of streams so blocking cannot occur.
column4: HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple independent streams over a single TCP connection with per-stream flow control.
column5: HTTP/2 uses UDP instead of TCP to avoid head-of-line blocking.
column6: HTTP/2 requires the browser to open exactly two TCP connections per host.

column2: An instant-messaging app must show new messages as soon as they arrive. Why is WebSocket preferable to HTTP polling for this use case?
column3: WebSocket operates without TCP; polling requires a TCP connection for every message.
column4: WebSocket lets the server push messages over a persistent bidirectional connection; polling requires repeated HTTP round-trips with significant overhead.
column5: Polling is always faster because each HTTP request is independent and lightweight.
column6: WebSocket only works over HTTPS; polling works over plain HTTP as well.

column2: A script loaded from foo.com tries to fetch data from bar.com. The Same-Origin Policy normally blocks this. What must happen to allow it?
column3: foo.com must add an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header to its outgoing requests.
column4: bar.com must include Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://foo.com in its responses to the browser's OPTIONS preflight request.
column5: The user must manually approve the cross-origin request in browser settings.
column6: Nothing — the Same-Origin Policy only blocks POST requests; GET is always allowed.
